1: A dramatic theophany: the divine warrior returns from Edom in blood-red garments, striding in his greatness, declaring his own righteousness (tsedaqah, disp 3) and his great power of salvation. The question-and-answer dialogue (Who is this? — I, speaking in righteousness) is a covenant-court exchange. The red garments of the warrior anticipate the winepress image of vv 2-3. Tsedaqah is simultaneously the character of the warrior and the verdict he enacts — his victory is righteous because it restores right order.
2: The garments reddened as from a winepress provoke the question. The winepress-treading is both literal (the harvested grapes) and metaphorical (the trampling of nations in judgment). The image recurs in Revelation 19:15, where the rider on the white horse treads the winepress of YHWH's wrath — the same divine warrior identified with the returning Messiah.
3: YHWH declares: I trod the winepress alone — from the nations no one helped me. The aloneness echoes the Servant's isolation in Isaiah 53:3-6. I trod them in my anger and trampled them in my fury; their blood spattered on my garments. The judicial violence is YHWH's own act, unassisted, against the nations that oppressed his people. The fury is covenant anger — the wrath of one who has been rejected in covenant.
4: The day of vengeance was in my heart, and the year of my redemption had arrived — the same paired terms as Isaiah 61:2, but now enacted. The day of vengeance in the heart of God: YHWH did not act impulsively but at the determined moment. The year of redemption is the same ratson-year of Isaiah 61:2 — the two are inseparable: favor to the afflicted and vengeance on the oppressor happen simultaneously.
5: YHWH looked and there was no one to help — the same astonishment as Isaiah 59:16, where no one intervened and YHWH's own arm brought salvation. I was appalled that there was none to sustain me; so my own arm brought salvation and my fury upheld me. The divine self-sufficiency in redemption is total: YHWH does not partner with human power. The arm of YHWH is the repeated image of the whole of Isaiah 40-66.
6: I trampled the peoples in my anger, made them drunk in my fury, and poured their blood down to the ground. The intoxication-image (cf. Isaiah 51:17-23: the cup of wrath) is now given to the oppressor-nations. What Zion drank in judgment, YHWH now forces on those who tormented Zion. The cosmic justice is complete: the one who handed out the cup of wrath now drains it himself as the oppressor.
7: The hymn of YHWH's lovingkindnesses (hesed, disp 4) — the speaker now turns from the theophany to recount YHWH's past faithfulness. The plural hasde YHWH (lovingkindnesses of YHWH, disp 4) emphasizes the accumulation of covenant-faithful acts over Israel's history. All that YHWH has bestowed on Israel, the great goodness according to his compassion and hesed (disp 4) — the double hesed at the opening of the hymn establishes that YHWH's character is the ground of his acts. The YHWH (disp 4) repeated three times in this verse underscores that this is personal-covenant love, not abstract divine benevolence.
8: YHWH said: Surely they are my people, children who will not prove false — and so he became their Savior. The divine self-commitment to Israel is based on what YHWH declared, not on what Israel delivered. The false-not-proving is what YHWH hoped for; what he received instead is the subject of vv 9-10. But the Savior-relationship was constituted by the declaration, not revoked by Israel's subsequent unfaithfulness.
9: In all their affliction he was afflicted (tsar lo) — the divine solidarity with Israel's suffering. The angel of his presence (phanaw — face) saved them. In his love (ahav) and compassion (hemlah) he redeemed them; he lifted them and carried them all the ancient days (kol yeme olam, disp 3). The carrying-image echoes Isaiah 40:11 (shepherd carrying lambs) and Isaiah 46:3-4 (YHWH carrying Israel from birth). The olam-carrying is not a moment but a sustained history of divine solidarity.
10: But they rebelled and grieved his Holy Spirit (ruach qodesh, ruach disp 4, qodesh disp 2) — the first occurrence of the exact phrase Holy Spirit in the Hebrew Bible (occurring here and v 11). The rebellion turned YHWH against them as their enemy. Grieving the Spirit is the language Paul uses in Ephesians 4:30 — the same theological category applied to the covenant community's relation to the divine presence. The Holy Spirit was the gift of YHWH's indwelling; its grieving is the rupture of intimacy.
11: Then he remembered the ancient days, the time of Moses and his people. Where is the one who brought them up from the sea with the shepherds of his flock? Where is the one who put his Holy Spirit (ruach qodesh, ruach disp 4, qodesh disp 2) among them? The prayer invokes the exodus precedent: YHWH who was with Moses and Israel in the wilderness is the same YHWH now. The Holy Spirit placed among the exodus community is the model for the Spirit-presence that vv 14 and Isaiah 61:1-3 promise. The remembering of YHWH (olam days, disp 3) is the basis for petitioning a new exodus.
12: Who led Moses by the right hand with his glorious arm, dividing the waters before them to make himself an everlasting (olam, disp 3) name — the exodus as the paradigmatic name-making event of YHWH. The olam-name is what YHWH's self-revelation in the exodus established permanently. The prayer is: do again what made your name everlasting.
13: Who led them through the depths — like a horse in the desert, they did not stumble. The comparison of Israel-in-the-Red-Sea to a horse in open terrain: in a place of maximum danger (the depths), Israel moved with maximum freedom (no stumbling). The God who made a safe path through the sea can make a safe path through any terrain.
14: As cattle descend into a valley to rest, the Spirit of YHWH (ruach YHWH, ruach disp 4, YHWH disp 4) gave them rest. The Spirit that grieved in v 10 is the same Spirit that gave rest in the wilderness — the contrast is intentional. YHWH led his people through the wilderness by his Spirit to make himself a glorious (kabod) name. The prayer ground: you have done this before for your own name's sake; do it again.
15: Look down from heaven and see, from your holy (qodesh, disp 2) and glorious dwelling. Where are your zeal and your strength? The yearning of your heart and your compassion — are they withheld from me? The prayer invokes YHWH's holy dwelling only to lament that what comes from that dwelling — zeal, strength, compassion — seems absent. This is the lament-form of covenant prayer: calling YHWH to act in accordance with what he is.
16: You are our Father (av) — though Abraham does not know us and Israel does not acknowledge us; you, YHWH (YHWH, disp 4), are our Father; our Redeemer from of old (olam, disp 3) is your name. The appeal to divine fatherhood over ancestral fatherhood: even if the patriarchs are inaccessible (in death), YHWH's fatherhood is present and active. The olam-Redeemer: YHWH's redemptive character is not a new decision but his eternal name — what he has always been called.
17: YHWH, why do you let us stray from your ways and harden our hearts from fearing you? The boldness of the question: the community holds YHWH partially responsible for their own waywardness — not as an excuse but as a cry for the return of his sustaining grace. Turn back for the sake of your servants, the tribes of your inheritance. The servants and the tribal inheritance are the covenant vocabulary that grounds the petition.
18: Your holy people (qodesh, disp 2) possessed your sanctuary for a little while; now our adversaries have trampled it down. The brevity of the possession (a little while) is measured against the eternity of YHWH's olam-name (v 16). The trampled sanctuary is the specific wound that makes the prayer urgent. YHWH's holiness-designation for Israel and the temple that housed his holiness are both violated.
19: We have become like those you never ruled, like those who were never called by your name. The ultimate covenant-desolation: to be indistinguishable from those outside the covenant. The prayer is at its lowest point here, which prepares the way for the breakthrough petition of chapter 64: the tearing-open of the heavens.
